title: 41st International Kangaroo Mathematics Contest 2025 — Junior (Grades 9-10)
disclaimer: Official Math Kangaroo past paper. For personal educational practice only.

solutions.1: After folding the right grey strip over the first fold line, it covers the middle column; after folding the left grey strip over the second fold line, it overlaps the same column. Tracking which windows remain visible, the numbers 9 and 5 align with the transparent windows, giving a sum of 9 + 5 = 14.
solutions.2: New area = ½ × (1.5b) × (⅔h) = ½bh = original area, so the ratio is 1:1. Increasing the base by 50% and decreasing the height by one-third exactly cancel each other out.
solutions.3: Hexagon E is divided so that two of its six equal triangular segments are black (1/3) and three are white (1/2), with the remaining one-sixth shaded grey. Only option E satisfies both conditions simultaneously.
solutions.4: The third Thursday falls earliest when March 1 is a Thursday: then the Thursdays are the 1st, 8th, and 15th. So the earliest possible Kangaroo Day is March 15th.
solutions.5: The recipe uses 1 cup of rice and 1½ cups of water. Ahmed wants to use 1⅔ cups of rice, which is 5/3 times the recipe amount. He needs 5/3 × 1½ = 5/3 × 3/2 = 5/2 = 2½ cups of water.
solutions.6: Digits are 2, 0, 2, 5. Numbers greater than 2025: five starting with 2 (namely 2052, 2205, 2250, 2502, 2520) and three starting with 5 (5022, 5202, 5220), giving 5 + 3 = 8 different numbers.
solutions.7: Folding a square along its diagonal creates a 45-45-90 triangle. Folding one short leg onto the hypotenuse bisects the 45° angle at that vertex, creating a 22.5° fold. Angle AXC = 180° - 45° - 22.5° = 112.5°.
solutions.8: The number is 80XY. Divisibility by 9 requires 8+0+X+Y ≡ 0 (mod 9), so X+Y = 10. Divisibility by 8 requires the last three digits 0XY divisible by 8, so 10X+Y is divisible by 8. From X+Y=10, only X=6, Y=4 gives 10X+Y=64, which is divisible by 8. The product of the missing digits is 6×4 = 24.
solutions.9: Let t = total pets. Dogs = t−8, rabbits = t−5, cats = t−7. Since these must sum to t: (t−8)+(t−5)+(t−7) = t → 3t−20 = t → 2t = 20 → t = 10.
solutions.10: Since Q is on the circle, OQ is a radius = 10 cm. In square OPQR, OQ is the diagonal, so the side length s satisfies s√2 = 10, giving s = 5√2. Triangle PQR has a right angle at Q (interior angle of square), with legs PQ = QR = s. Area = ½ × s² = ½ × 50 = 25 cm².
solutions.11: Each photo shows 3 consecutive medals and contains exactly one gold. The 6 photos cover sets {1,2,3},{2,3,4},{3,4,5},{4,5,6},{5,6,7},{6,7,1}. Medal 3 appears in the first three photos and medal 6 appears in the last three — these two sets are disjoint, so assigning gold to 3 and 6 gives exactly one gold per photo. Sum = 3 + 6 = 9.
solutions.12: Original display: 16 wide × 9 tall. After rotating phone: display is 9 wide × 16 tall. The 16:9 picture scaled to fit width 9 has height 9×(9/16) = 81/16. Picture area = 9 × 81/16 = 729/16; display area = 9 × 16 = 144. Fraction = 729/(16×144) = 729/2304 = 81/256.
solutions.13: Let Khawlah's age = k, Ahmed's age = a. Given k/19 = a/17, so k = 19t and a = 17t for positive integer t. Sum = 36t must satisfy 40 < 36t < 100, forcing t = 2. Khawlah's age = 19×2 = 38.
solutions.14: Let Basem shoot a times at top-left and b = 27−a at bottom-right. Misses: 0.5a + 0.2(27−a) = 9 → 0.3a = 3.6 → a = 12. Hits at top-left = 0.5 × 12 = 6.
solutions.15: Primes from 1 to 18: 2, 3, 5, 7, 11, 13, 17 (seven primes). Non-primes: 1, 4, 6, 8, 9, 10, 12, 14, 15, 16, 18 (eleven values). In the worst case Sara removes all 11 non-primes and 2 primes (13 balls) before guaranteeing 3 primes. She must remove at least 14 balls to guarantee having at least 3 primes.
solutions.16: The shape has 8 cells arranged so that cell X's neighbors include cells adjacent by side or vertex. For consecutive numbers not to share a cell adjacent to X, only digits 2 and 7 — which are not adjacent (in value) to the forced placements in neighboring cells — can be placed in X.
solutions.17: Factor 180 = 4 × 5 × 9 × 1 × 1 × 1. To maximize the six-digit number, arrange digits in descending order: 9, 5, 4, 1, 1, 1 → number 954111. The digit sum = 9+5+4+1+1+1 = 21.
solutions.18: Place the two congruent shaded rectangles (each area 4) inside ABCD with their corners touching the sides of ABCD. Setting up equations from the overlap constraint gives the side lengths of ABCD such that area = 8√3. The perimeter follows from the rectangle's dimensions.
solutions.19: One prime must be 11 (since pqr = 11(p+q+r) requires 11 | product). Let primes be 11, q, r: then qr − q − r = 11 → (q−1)(r−1) = 12. The factor pair (1,12) gives q=2, r=13, sum = 11+2+13 = 26 (maximum).
solutions.20: The five bricks form a staircase with specific removal constraints. By symmetry and counting valid orderings where brick 4 is exactly the third removed, the probability equals 1/4.
solutions.21: The grey rectangle is 7×5 and the striped rectangle is 8×7, both inside square ABCD with overlap area 18 cm². Setting the square's side length to s, the overlap dimensions are (12−s) × (15−s) = 18, giving s² − 27s + 162 = 0. Solving: s = 9 (the value less than 12). Perimeter = 4×9 = 36 cm.
solutions.22: ABCD × D = DXYA requires last digit D² ≡ A (mod 10) and the product to be a 4-digit number starting with D. Testing all digits 1–9, only D = 9 works (A = 1, product ≤ 9999). The valid numbers are 1009, 1019, …, 1099 (ten) and 1109 (one), giving 11 four-digit integers.
solutions.23: Labeling squares in a counter-clockwise spiral with side 0.5 cm: after k complete layers the shape is a (2k+1)×(2k+1) square. Since 1 + 4k(k+1) = 2025 gives (2k+1)² = 2025, so 2k+1 = 45. The shape is a 45×45 grid of 0.5 cm squares, so each side = 22.5 cm and perimeter = 4 × 22.5 = 90 cm.
solutions.24: Since E must be 5 (only way ABCDE is divisible by 5 using digits 1–6), the remaining digits {1,2,3,4,6} fill A,B,C,D,F. B and F must be even; AB divisible by 2; ABC divisible by 3; CD divisible by 4; ABCDEF divisible by 6. Systematic check yields F = 4 in every valid arrangement.
solutions.25: Let outer radius = R and inner radius = r. The chord of length 16 parallel to the outer diameter is tangent to the inner circle, so its distance from the outer center equals r. Chord-distance formula gives R² − r² = 64. The shaded area = π(R² − r²) = 64π.
solutions.26: For n ≥ 3, each term equals the mean of all previous terms. This forces all terms from a₃ onward to equal S₂/2 where S₂ = a₁ + a₂. Since a₁₀ = S₂/2 = 26, we get S₂ = 52. Then a₂ = 52 − a₁ = 52 − 8 = 44.
solutions.27: The 6 circles form a triangle with one on each corner and one on each side. The sum per side S satisfies 3S = (vertex sum) + 21. For S to be an integer, vertex sums must be ≡ 0 (mod 3), giving vertex sums 6, 9, 12, and 15 — each verifiably achievable. So there are 4 possible values.
solutions.28: Three pairs of twins must each wear the same color. Let x twin-pairs wear blue (0 to 3): the remaining 6 non-twins get C(6, 6−2x) blue hats. Total = Σ C(3,x)·C(6,2x) = 1 + 45 + 45 + 1 = 92.
solutions.29: Using barycentric coordinates: I = midpoint of BC = (0,½,½), and J = (⅔, 0, ⅓) (one-third of the way from A to C). Lines AI and BJ intersect at L = (½, ¼, ¼). The area ratio of triangle ALJ to ABC equals |det[A, L, J]| = 1/12. Area = 60/12 = 5.
solutions.30: The number of ways to fill a 2×4 grid with 1–8 increasing rightward and downward equals the number of standard Young tableaux of shape (4,4). By the hook-length formula: 8! ÷ (5×4×3×2×4×3×2×1) = 40320 ÷ 2880 = 14.
